answer.rationale: The simpliciter doctrine that Christ died on behalf of and in place of sinners is canonically demonstrated by the convergence of Isa 53:4-6 (paga H6293 hiphil, YHWH causes the iniquity of us all to fall on him), Mark 10:45 (lytron anti pollon G3083 + G473, ransom in-place-of many), and the dense Pauline-Petrine hyper G5228 network (Rom 5:6-8, 2 Cor 5:14-21, Gal 3:13, 1 Pet 2:24, 1 Pet 3:18, Heb 9:28), with 1 Cor 15:3 making 'Christ died for our sins' the gospel summary; the prepositions anti and hyper plus the substitutionary lytron noun carry the verdict at the lexical level.
answer.notes: Critical distinction: this question is about substitutionary atonement simpliciter (Christ died for us, in our place), NOT about the specific Reformed penal-satisfaction THEORY of how that substitution works. The simpliciter doctrine is universally affirmed across every historic Christian lineage (patristic Christus-Victor, Anselmian satisfaction, Catholic-magisterial, Lutheran-sacramental, Anglican Article 2, Reformed penal-substitution, Methodist, Anabaptist, Pentecostal, Eastern Orthodox). The internal disagreement is about MECHANISM (Christus-Victor stresses triumph over death, devil, and sin; satisfaction stresses payment of debt to divine honor; penal-substitution stresses bearing the penalty in our place; governmental stresses public demonstration of justice). All these mechanisms presuppose the simpliciter substitution. Public-record carriers of denial: classical Abelardian moral-exemplar-only views, modern liberal-mainline reductions that reduce the cross to martyrdom or moral example only, some 'non-violent' atonement proposals (Weaver, some feminist and womanist reductions) that explicitly reject substitution. The Socinians historically denied substitution as 'unjust'. Unitarian Universalists generally reject it. To deny that Christ died FOR us in any substitutionary sense empties 1 Cor 15:3 and severs the explicit hyper / anti / paga lexical pattern of Scripture; that is a gospel-stake denial. Brethren tradition holds this strongly and rightly, but so does the entire historic church; this is not a Brethren distinctive.

stem_audit.neutralized_form: Christ died on behalf of and in place of sinners, such that his death has substitutionary force for those for whom he died.
stem_audit.notes: The original statement uses 'as a substitute, bearing the penalty for the sins of His people in their place', which fuses two things: (a) vicarious substitution simpliciter (universally held), and (b) penal-satisfaction language ('bearing the penalty') plus a particular-redemption qualifier ('His people'). Both (b) elements are confessional Reformed phrasings, not lexically neutral. The neutralized form keeps the substitutionary core that the Greek prepositions hyper / anti and Hebrew paga carry, and leaves the mechanism and the extent of the atonement to separate questions (penal substitution proper is K2_explicitly_excluded as theory-specific; particular vs general extent is a distinct in-house debate).

lay_summary.reasoning: Yes, the Bible plainly teaches that Jesus died for us, in our place, taking what we deserved. The strongest reason is the simple weight of the prepositions across the New Testament. Mark 10:45 says the Son of Man came to give his life as a ransom 'in place of' many. Romans 5:8 says 'while we were still sinners, Christ died for us.' 2 Corinthians 5:21 says 'he made him who knew no sin to be sin for us, that we might become the righteousness of God in him.' Galatians 3:13 says Christ became 'a curse for us.' 1 Peter 2:24 says he 'bore our sins in his body on the tree.' 1 Peter 3:18 says 'the righteous for the unrighteous, that he might bring us to God.' This pattern is everywhere. The Old Testament foundation is Isaiah 53, where the servant is pierced for our transgressions, crushed for our iniquities, and the Lord 'has laid on him the iniquity of us all.' The strongest counterargument comes from texts that emphasize Christ as moral example or victor over evil rather than substitute. 1 Peter 2:21 calls Christ an example for us to follow. Colossians 2:15 says he disarmed the powers, triumphing over them. Some readers argue these mean the cross is mainly example or mainly victory, not substitution. But these texts do not deny substitution. They sit alongside it. The same 1 Peter 2 that calls Christ an example also says in the next breath that he bore our sins. The cross is example AND victory AND substitution. Critics like Abelard, some modern liberal theologians, and some Unitarian streams reject substitution as unjust or barbaric. Their position breaks Scripture's own pattern. Every historic Christian tradition affirms that Christ died for us in our place, though they disagree about the mechanism of how that substitution works.
lay_summary.denominational_landscape: Every historic Christian lineage affirms that Christ died for us, in our place, in some substitutionary sense. The patristic writers, especially Athanasius in 'On the Incarnation', describe Christ surrendering his body 'instead of all' and offering it to the Father; Gregory of Nyssa and the early Greek fathers also emphasize a ransom paid on our behalf. Anselm's 'Cur Deus Homo' formalized the satisfaction view in the medieval West, arguing that Christ's death pays the debt humanity owed. The Catholic Catechism teaches that Christ delivered himself up for our sins and that his redemptive death is the heart of God's plan of salvation. Lutheran teaching (Augsburg Confession Article III) says Christ was 'a sacrifice, not only for original guilt, but also for all actual sins of men.' Anglican teaching (39 Articles, Article 2) says Christ suffered 'to reconcile his Father to us, and to be a sacrifice... for all actual sins of men.' Methodist Articles of Religion repeat the same language verbatim. Reformed and Baptist confessions teach penal substitution as the dominant mechanism. Pentecostal and Assemblies of God statements affirm Christ's vicarious atonement. Eastern Orthodoxy emphasizes Christus-Victor (Christ's triumph over death, the devil, and corruption) and rejects strict penal-satisfaction language, but it strongly affirms that Christ died for us, redeems us, and offers himself in our place. Anabaptist confessions (Dordrecht) affirm Christ's atoning sacrifice. The lineages disagree about the mechanism, not the simpliciter substitution. Public-record denials sit outside historic Christianity. Socinians (16th-17th c.) rejected substitution as unjust. Modern liberal-mainline theology in its reductive form treats the cross as moral example only, following Abelard. Some feminist and womanist theologians (e.g., Weaver's nonviolent atonement, certain Brown and Parker arguments) reject substitution. Unitarian Universalists generally reject it. Jehovah's Witnesses affirm a 'ransom' but reframe it in non-trinitarian terms. Iglesia ni Cristo denies the deity of the one who atones, which collapses the substitution. Mormons affirm atonement but locate it primarily in Gethsemane and within a different ontology. Outright denial that Christ died for us in our place severs the explicit lexical pattern of Scripture and removes the substance of 1 Corinthians 15:3.

scripture.key_term: נָשָׂא (nasa) H5375 + סָבַל (sabal) H5445 + פָּגַע (paga) H6293 hiphil + עָוֹן (avon) H5771
scripture.force: He has borne (nasa) our griefs and carried (sabal) our sorrows; YHWH caused to meet upon him (hiphil of paga) the iniquity (avon) of us all. The hiphil of paga is causative: YHWH actively transfers iniquity onto the servant. Direct lexical substitution.
His soul makes an asham (guilt offering); he bore the sin of many and made intercession (paga) for the transgressors. Sacrificial-substitution language explicit.

The Son of Man came to give his life as a ransom in-place-of (anti) many. Anti carries strict substitutionary force; lytron is the redemption-price paid to release another. This is the most explicit substitution saying of Jesus.
Parallel saying to Mark 10:45; doubles the canonical weight of the substitution declaration on Jesus's own lips.

Christ died for (hyper) the ungodly; while we were still sinners Christ died for us. Hyper here carries representative and substitutionary force; the contrast with our state (ungodly, sinners) makes the in-our-place reading necessary.
One died for (hyper) all, therefore all died. The logic only works if his death counts as theirs, that is, substitutionary.
He made him who knew no sin to be sin for us (hyper hemon), so that in him we might become the righteousness of God. Direct exchange formula: he gets our status, we get his.

scripture.key_term: κατάρα (katara) G2671 + ὑπέρ G5228 + ἐξαγοράζω (exagorazo) G1805
scripture.force: Christ redeemed us out of the curse of the law, having become a curse for us (hyper hemon). The curse he absorbs is the one we owed under Deut 21:23.
He himself bore our sins in his body on the tree. Anaphero is the LXX sacrificial verb (bringing offerings up to the altar); Peter applies it to Christ carrying our sins.
Christ suffered once for sins, the righteous for (hyper) the unrighteous, to bring us to God. Maximally explicit substitution: the righteous one in place of the unrighteous ones.
Christ was offered once to bear (anaphero) the sins of many. Echoes Isa 53:12 directly; LXX sacrificial vocabulary applied to Christ.

By that will we have been sanctified through the offering of the body of Jesus Christ once for all; by a single offering he has perfected for all time those who are being sanctified. Sacrificial-substitution language tied to single definitive offering.
He is the propitiation (hilasmos) for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the sins of the whole world. Hilasmos is sacrificial-atonement language carrying substitutionary force.
Herein is love, not that we loved God, but that he loved us and sent his Son to be the propitiation (hilasmos) for our sins. Love and substitutionary propitiation are joined, not opposed.
Christ died for (hyper) our sins according to the Scriptures. Paul's gospel summary; received tradition; substitutionary force foundational.
The good shepherd lays down his life for (hyper) the sheep. Voluntary substitution.
Caiaphas prophesies that one man should die for (hyper) the people; John reads this as substitutionary atonement.
Whom God set forth as a hilasterion (mercy seat / propitiation) in his blood. Day-of-Atonement typology applied to Christ; sacrificial-substitution.
Aaron lays both hands on the scapegoat, confesses over it all the iniquities of Israel, and the goat bears (nasa) all their iniquities upon him to a remote land. Foundational substitution typology.
Christ suffered for you, leaving you an example to follow in his steps. Some take this as the example-only counter; but the very next verse (2:24) names him as sin-bearer. Example AND substitute.

Disarmed the rulers and authorities, made a public spectacle of them, triumphing over them in him. Christus-Victor strand; complicates a substitution-only frame but does not deny substitution (the very next breath in Col 2:14 names the legal certificate cancelled at the cross).

Through death he destroyed the one who has the power of death, that is, the devil, and delivered all those who through fear of death were subject to lifelong slavery. Christus-Victor; sits alongside substitution.

competing_lens_verdicts.note: Substitution is the heart of covenantal atonement, with Christ as the second Adam taking the covenantal curse.
Substitution is foundational to dispensational soteriology, present in every dispensation typologically.
Christ's substitutionary death inaugurates the new covenant predicted in Jer 31.
Substitution climaxes the Levitical typology in the antitype Christ.
Affirms substitution simpliciter (Christ dies on our behalf) but emphasizes the dominant mechanism as triumph over death, devil, and sin rather than penal payment.

hermeneutics.notes: All frameworks converge on substitution simpliciter. They diverge on the dominant mechanism (penal-satisfaction vs Christus-Victor vs governmental vs recapitulation). The simpliciter verdict does not require choosing among these; it requires only the lexical reading of hyper, anti, paga, anaphero, hilasmos, and lytron. The mechanism question is in-house and handled separately under doc-penal-substitution (theory-specific, K2_explicitly_excluded).

counter_witness.key_phrase: He surrendered His body to death instead of all, and offered it to the Father. By the sacrifice of His own body He put an end to the law of death.

counter_witness.anchor: Gregory of Nyssa, Great Catechism, ch. 22-24
counter_witness.key_phrase: Christ paid a ransom on behalf of humanity, taking the place of those held in bondage to death; substitution simpliciter with Christus-Victor mechanism.

counter_witness.anchor: Catechism of the Catholic Church 601-605, 'Christ's redemptive death in God's plan of salvation'
counter_witness.key_phrase: Christ died for our sins according to the scriptures. By giving his life as a ransom for many, Jesus accomplished the plan of God; he offered himself in our place.

counter_witness.anchor: Anselm, Cur Deus Homo, Bk II ch. 6-7
counter_witness.key_phrase: It is necessary that the God-man pay what is owed by the sinful human race; he alone could make satisfaction sufficient for the debt of sin.

counter_witness.anchor: Augsburg Confession, Article III, Of the Son of God
counter_witness.key_phrase: That He might reconcile the Father unto us, and be a sacrifice, not only for original guilt, but also for all actual sins of men.

counter_witness.anchor: 39 Articles, Article 2, Of the Word or Son of God which was made very Man
counter_witness.key_phrase: Christ truly suffered, was crucified, dead, and buried, to reconcile his Father to us, and to be a sacrifice, not only for original guilt, but also for all actual sins of men.

The Lord Jesus, by his perfect obedience and sacrifice of himself, hath fully satisfied the justice of God, procuring reconciliation and an everlasting inheritance for those whom the Father hath given him.

counter_witness.anchor: UMC Articles of Religion, Article II, Article XX
counter_witness.key_phrase: Christ truly suffered, was crucified, dead, and buried, to reconcile his Father to us, and to be a sacrifice. The offering of Christ, once made, is that perfect redemption, propitiation, and satisfaction for all the sins of the whole world.
Christ has, through his precious blood and death on the cross, atoned for the sins of the whole human race, accomplishing the redemption of fallen humanity.

counter_witness.anchor: Assemblies of God, Statement of Fundamental Truths, Art. 3 (Deity of Christ) and Art. 5 (Salvation of Man)
counter_witness.key_phrase: Man's only hope of redemption is through the shed blood of Jesus Christ the Son of God; his vicarious and atoning death is the only sufficient ground of justification.

counter_witness.anchor: OCA, The Orthodox Faith, Volume 1, Doctrine: The Crucifixion
counter_witness.key_phrase: Christ died for us. Orthodox tradition rejects strict penal-satisfaction language but affirms that the Son of God voluntarily took our place, suffered for our salvation, and conquered death by death.

web.quote: G5228 hyper: for, on behalf of. One who does a thing for another is conceived as standing or bending over the one whom he would shield or defend. Substitutionary force.
G473 anti: opposite, instead of, in place of. Mark 10:45 his life a ransom for many: anti anchors substitutionary atonement, divine exchange.
G3083 lytron: ransom, redemption price; the price paid to free a slave. Two occurrences, both on Jesus's lips: Matt 20:28 and Mark 10:45.
lytron anti pollon: ransom in place of many. Anti carries substitutionary exchange; the Son of Man gives his life as the redemption price.
hiphil of paga H6293: YHWH caused the iniquity of us all to fall upon him. Causative voice; divine action of transferring guilt to the servant.
ton me gnonta hamartian hyper hemon hamartian epoiesen: the one who knew no sin he made to be sin for us. Direct substitution formula.
genomenos hyper hemon katara: having become for us a curse. Christ takes the curse owed by us under the law's penalty.
tas hamartias hemon autos anenenken: he himself bore our sins. Anenenken is the LXX sacrificial verb of carrying offerings to the altar.
Augsburg Article III: that He might reconcile the Father unto us, and be a sacrifice, not only for original guilt, but also for all actual sins of men.
UMC Article XX: The offering of Christ, once made, is that perfect redemption, propitiation, and satisfaction for all the sins of the whole world.
Athanasius: He surrendered His body to death instead of all, and offered it to the Father. The sacrifice of His own body put an end to the law of death.
